learning_objectives: Define sociology and the sociological perspective
Apply C. Wright Mills's sociological imagination to personal troubles and public issues
Compare the three major sociological paradigms: functionalism, conflict theory, symbolic interactionism
readings: Giddens & Sutton, Sociology, Ch. 1; Mills, The Sociological Imagination, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Sociology asks how society shapes individual experience. The sociological imagination -- the ability to see the connection between personal biography and larger social structures -- is the discipline's most powerful tool.
key_concepts: Sociological imagination: personal troubles vs. public issues
Functionalism (Durkheim): society as an organism with interdependent parts serving functions
Conflict theory (Marx, Weber): society as an arena of inequality and power struggle
Symbolic interactionism (Mead, Blumer): meaning is constructed through social interaction
Sociology as science: empirical evidence, systematic observation, theory testing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the sociological imagination to unemployment: what appears as a personal failure is shaped by economic structures, education systems, and policy

review.value: Mills: the ability to connect individual biography to larger social forces; understanding that personal troubles often reflect public issues
Functionalism asks 'how does this contribute to social stability?'; Conflict theory asks 'who benefits and who is harmed?'; Symbolic interactionism asks 'how do people create meaning together?'

learning_objectives: Define culture and distinguish material from non-material culture
Describe the agents of socialization and their influence across the lifespan
Explain social construction of reality and its implications

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture is the lens through which we see the world. Socialization is the process by which we acquire that lens. Both are so pervasive that they feel natural rather than constructed.
key_concepts: Material culture (artifacts) vs. non-material culture (values, norms, beliefs)
Ethnocentrism vs. cultural relativism
Agents of socialization: family, peers, education, media, religion
Looking-glass self (Cooley): self-concept reflects others' perceptions
Social construction of reality (Berger & Luckmann): society is a human product, and humans are social products
lecture_notes.demonstration: Breaching experiment discussion: what happens when taken-for-granted norms are violated (Garfinkel)?

review.value: Berger & Luckmann: reality is not given but actively created through social interaction; what seems natural (gender roles, race categories) is historically and culturally contingent
Cooley: we develop our sense of self by imagining how we appear to others, imagining their judgment, and feeling pride or shame accordingly

learning_objectives: Define social stratification and compare class-based systems of inequality
Describe the relationship between socioeconomic status and life outcomes
Evaluate theories of inequality: functionalist (Davis-Moore) vs. conflict (Marx, Weber)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every society is stratified -- organized into layers of unequal access to resources, opportunities, and power. Understanding stratification is central to sociology.
key_concepts: Social stratification: systematic, enduring inequality built into social structures
Class systems vs. caste systems vs. slavery
Weber's three dimensions: class (economics), status (prestige), party (power)
Davis-Moore thesis: inequality is functional because it motivates talented people to fill important positions
Marx's critique: inequality benefits those at the top and exploits those at the bottom
lecture_notes.demonstration: Inequality simulation: distribute resources unequally in a game and observe behavioral and attitudinal consequences

review.value: Class = economic resources; status = social honor/prestige; party = political power. These are related but not identical: a teacher may have high status but low class position

Functional theory: unequal rewards motivate the most talented to undertake the most important, difficult positions. Critique: ignores inherited advantage, discrimination, and the role of power in defining 'important' positions

learning_objectives: Distinguish race as a social construction from ethnicity as cultural identity
Describe the levels of racism: individual, institutional, structural
Analyze the persistence of racial inequality through sociological frameworks
readings: Giddens & Sutton, Ch. 15; Bonilla-Silva, Racism Without Racists, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Race is a social construction with profound real consequences. Sociology reveals how racial inequality is maintained through institutional processes, not just individual prejudice.
key_concepts: Race as a social construction: no biological basis for racial categories, but powerful social consequences
Institutional racism: policies and practices that produce unequal outcomes regardless of intent
Structural racism: interconnecting institutions that reproduce racial hierarchy
Color-blind racism (Bonilla-Silva): racial inequality is maintained through frames that avoid explicit racial language
Intersectionality (Crenshaw): race interacts with gender, class, and other axes of inequality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze racial disparities in a specific domain (housing, criminal justice, healthcare) using institutional and structural racism frameworks

review.value: Policies that produce racial disparities without requiring individual racial animus: e.g., school funding tied to property values, criminal sentencing guidelines, hiring practices relying on personal networks
Crenshaw: categories of identity (race, gender, class, sexuality) interact to create unique experiences of privilege and oppression that cannot be understood by examining any single category alone

learning_objectives: Distinguish sex, gender, and sexuality as sociological concepts
Describe how gender is socially constructed and institutionally reinforced
Analyze major social institutions (family, education, religion) using sociological theory

lecture_notes.introduction: Gender is not simply biology -- it is a social institution that organizes every aspect of life from childhood socialization to occupational structure to intimate relationships.
key_concepts: Sex (biological) vs. gender (social) vs. sexuality (desire and identity)
Gender as performance (Butler): gender is not something we are but something we do
Gender socialization across institutions: family, school, media, peers
Gender stratification: wage gap, occupational segregation, political representation
Sociology of the family: changing structures, diversity, and inequality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Content analysis exercise: analyze children's media for gender role messages

review.value: Butler: gender is not an innate identity but a repeated performance of culturally scripted behaviors; we 'do gender' through dress, speech, gesture, and interaction

The gender wage gap (women earn ~82 cents per dollar) reflects occupational segregation, caregiving expectations, discrimination, and negotiation differences -- structural forces, not just individual choices

class_schedule.topic: Deviance, Social Control, and Social Change
learning_objectives: Apply labeling theory and strain theory to deviant behavior
Describe mechanisms of social control: formal and informal
Discuss theories of social change: globalization, social movements, technology

lecture_notes.introduction: Deviance is not inherent in an act but in the social reaction to it. Understanding how societies define, control, and respond to deviance reveals fundamental dynamics of power and social order.
key_concepts: Labeling theory (Becker): deviance is not a quality of the act but the result of societal reaction
Strain theory (Merton): deviance results from the gap between culturally valued goals and available means
Social control: informal (norms, gossip, shaming) and formal (laws, police, courts)
Social movements: resource mobilization, political process, framing theories
Globalization: economic integration, cultural exchange, and inequality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debate: is marijuana legalization a case of changing norms (labeling theory) or rational policy (strain theory)?

review.value: Becker: 'deviant behavior is behavior that people so label.' A marijuana user is deviant in one society and normal in another. Labels create secondary deviance through stigma and self-fulfilling prophecy
Merton: when society promotes goals (wealth) but restricts means (education, jobs), people adapt through conformity, innovation, ritualism, retreatism, or rebellion

questions.question: Using C. Wright Mills's sociological imagination, reframe the personal trouble of student loan debt as a public issue. Identify at least two structural forces that contribute to the problem.
questions.answer: The sociological imagination transforms student debt from an individual failure to save or choose wisely into a public issue shaped by structural forces: (1) Defunding of public higher education: state funding for universities has declined dramatically, shifting costs to students through tuition increases. (2) The credential inflation of the labor market: jobs that previously required only a high school diploma now demand college degrees, making higher education nearly mandatory for middle-class employment. Additional structural forces: predatory lending practices, rising cost of living, wage stagnation, and the financialization of higher education. The individual debtor is caught in a system that simultaneously requires a degree and makes it financially devastating.

rubric.key: Correctly applies sociological imagination (personal trouble to public issue)
Identifies two structural forces with explanation
Demonstrates understanding of the connection between biography and history

questions.question: Compare how a functionalist and a conflict theorist would explain the existence of social stratification. Which perspective do you find more convincing and why?
questions.answer: Functionalist (Davis-Moore): Stratification is necessary and beneficial because it ensures that the most talented people fill the most important positions by offering higher rewards. Inequality motivates achievement and serves society's needs. Conflict theorist (Marx): Stratification exists because those with power structure society to maintain their advantages. Wealth is inherited, not earned; 'important' positions are defined by those already in power; and the system exploits workers while benefiting owners. The conflict perspective is more convincing because: the Davis-Moore thesis cannot explain inherited wealth, discrimination, or why CEOs earn 300x worker salaries. Many essential positions (teachers, nurses) are poorly compensated, undermining the functionalist claim that rewards match social importance.

rubric.key: Provides reasoned evaluation with supporting argument

questions.question: Explain the concept of institutional racism and provide one specific example showing how a policy or practice can produce racial disparities without requiring individual prejudice.
questions.answer: Institutional racism refers to policies, practices, and procedures within organizations and social institutions that produce unequal outcomes by race, regardless of the intentions of the individuals involved. Example: School funding tied to local property taxes. This policy is race-neutral on its face -- it does not mention race. However, because of historical patterns of residential segregation (themselves the product of redlining, restrictive covenants, and discriminatory lending), communities of color have lower property values and therefore less school funding. The result: predominantly Black and Latino schools receive less money per pupil, leading to fewer resources, worse facilities, less experienced teachers, and lower educational outcomes -- all without any individual school board member needing to harbor racial prejudice.

rubric.key: Provides specific example with clear mechanism
Shows how racial disparities result without individual prejudice

questions.question: Apply labeling theory to explain how a juvenile who commits a minor offense can become a career criminal. What role does the label play in this transformation?
questions.answer: Labeling theory (Becker, Lemert) explains this through the concepts of primary and secondary deviance. Primary deviance: the initial minor offense (e.g., shoplifting) -- common among adolescents and usually temporary. Labeling: if the juvenile is caught and formally labeled (arrested, adjudicated, recorded), they are publicly identified as 'delinquent.' This label changes how others respond: teachers lower expectations, peers reject or reinforce deviant identity, employers refuse to hire. Secondary deviance: the person internalizes the label ('I am a criminal'), their self-concept shifts, and they increasingly act in accordance with the label. They may join deviant peer groups (the only group that accepts them), further reinforcing criminal behavior. The label creates a self-fulfilling prophecy: the societal reaction to the initial act produces the very criminality it claims to merely identify.

rubric.key: Correctly explains primary and secondary deviance
Describes the labeling mechanism and its social consequences

major_assignments.title: Sociological Analysis of a Social Issue
major_assignments.description: Select a contemporary social issue (e.g., homelessness, mass incarceration, educational inequality, healthcare access) and analyze it using at least two sociological perspectives. Include empirical data, apply relevant sociological concepts, and propose evidence-based policy recommendations.
objectives: Apply sociological theories to a real-world social issue
Use empirical data to support sociological analysis
Demonstrate understanding of how social structures produce inequality
Propose policy recommendations grounded in sociological evidence
deliverables: 3,000-word analytical paper in ASA or APA format
Application of at least two sociological perspectives
Empirical data from reputable sources (Census, BLS, CDC)
Minimum 10 references including academic and data sources

criteria: At least two theoretical perspectives applied
Addresses root causes, not just symptoms
Sophisticated sociological analysis using multiple perspectives
Strong empirical evidence from reputable sources
Evidence-based policy recommendations addressing root causes

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of sociological concepts and theories

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions and activities

major_assignments.description: Sociological analysis of a contemporary social issue

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering sociological imagination through stratification

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all sociological topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Participation in discussions is a graded component.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Extensions require advance notice with documentation.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and debate are encouraged. All written work must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Data must be from legitimate sources.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to university LMS, word processing software, and data sources (Census Bureau, BLS).
